result:
# The Curious Appeal of Liminal Spaces

There's something oddly captivating about empty parking garages at 2 AM, abandoned shopping malls frozen in 1987, or fluorescent-lit hotel hallways that seem to stretch into infinity. These "liminal spaces," as the internet has dubbed them, have exploded in popularity over the past few years, spawning countless image boards, YouTube videos, and even aesthetic movements. But what exactly makes these transitional, in-between places so fascinating to us?

The term "liminal" comes from the Latin word "limen," meaning threshold. Anthropologists originally used it to describe the disorienting middle stage of rituals—that moment when you've left your old status behind but haven't yet arrived at your new one. You're between worlds, neither here nor there. Modern liminal spaces capture this same essence, but architecturally. They're the places we pass through rather than destinations themselves: hallways, stairwells, empty airports at dawn, highway rest stops, or that weird transitional area between shopping mall stores.

What strikes me most about our collective obsession with these spaces is how it reveals something fundamental about human psychology and memory. These aren't beautiful places in any conventional sense. Nobody designs a parking garage to be aesthetically moving. Yet photographs of empty ones, bathed in sodium vapor light, can evoke incredibly powerful emotions—usually an unsettling mixture of nostalgia, unease, and strange familiarity.

I think part of the appeal lies in the uncanny nature of seeing spaces without their people. These places are designed for human traffic, for movement and activity. When we see them empty and still, there's an wrongness to it, like stumbling upon a stage set after the play has ended and all the actors have gone home. The chairs are still arranged, the lights are still on, but the animating force—human presence—has vanished. It's a preview of what our world might look like after us, which carries both an eerie quality and a strange peace.

There's also something deeply nostalgic about many liminal space images, particularly those featuring 1980s and 90s architecture and design. For millennials and Gen Z, these spaces trigger memories—or pseudo-memories—of childhood. That roller rink with geometric carpet patterns. The swimming pool complex with too much echo. The pizza place with the strange lighting and arcade games in the back. Even if you never visited these specific places, they feel familiar because they shared a common design language that's now extinct.

This brings up a fascinating aspect of how we process memory and experience. Many people report feeling nostalgic for liminal spaces they're certain they've never actually been to. This phenomenon suggests that our memories work less like video recordings and more like impressionist paintings—we remember feelings, atmospheres, and general impressions rather than precise details. A fluorescent-lit hallway in an image might not be the exact hallway from your childhood dentist's office, but it's close enough that your brain fills in the rest, generating a false sense of recognition.

The internet has transformed how we experience and share these spaces. Before platforms like Reddit and TikTok, you might have occasionally felt that strange emotion when walking through an empty space at an odd hour, but you probably wouldn't have had a name for it or realized others felt the same way. Now there are massive communities dedicated to collecting and categorizing these experiences. The "Backrooms" creepypasta—an internet horror story about infinite, empty office spaces—emerged directly from this aesthetic and has spawned an entire fictional universe.

I find it telling that this aesthetic has flourished precisely when so much of our lives has moved online. As we spend more time in digital spaces, perhaps we're developing a new appreciation for physical places, even mundane ones. Or maybe it's the opposite—perhaps liminal spaces appeal to us because they feel like physical manifestations of how the internet actually works. After all, what is a website but a space you pass through? What is scrolling but moving through an endless, fluorescent-lit hallway of content?

There's also a meditative quality to contemplating these empty spaces. In our hyper-stimulated world, there's something almost radical about images that feature... nothing much. No people, no action, no obvious point. Just space existing for its own sake. It's anti-content content, images that ask you to slow down and feel rather than consume and move on.

The aesthetic has even influenced contemporary horror, which has moved away from jump scares and gore toward a more subtle, atmospheric dread. Games like "The Stanley Parable" and "Superliminal" use empty office spaces to create psychological unease. The horror isn't a monster jumping out—it's the wrongness of space itself, the feeling that something is slightly off, that you're not supposed to be here, or that you are here but you can't leave.

What fascinates me is how this represents a shift in what we find unsettling. Previous generations might have feared dark forests or abandoned houses—spaces marked by decay and obvious danger. We fear clean, well-lit spaces that simply lack human presence. Perhaps this reflects modern anxieties about isolation, about automation, about lives lived increasingly separate from physical community. The empty mall is scarier than the ruined castle because it represents a failure that feels more immediate and possible.

As I think about liminal spaces, I realize they've always been with us—we've just found new ways to see and share them. Every generation has its thresholds, its in-between places that capture something about how it feels to be alive in that particular moment. For us, it's the empty places of late capitalism, still humming with electricity, waiting for people who may never come back. There's melancholy in that, but also beauty in our ability to notice these overlooked spaces and find meaning in the meaningless. Even—or especially—in the space between.
